title: Daily Health Briefing
intro: Good morning! Here's today's research digest across your tracked topics. Each section summarizes recent, reputable findings — sources are linked as chips.

sections.body:
**Urolithin A (UA)** continues to look interesting for cellular/immune aging but not as a proven performance booster. A 2025 randomized, placebo-controlled trial (1,000 mg/day for 4 weeks in healthy middle-aged adults) found UA safely shifted immune-cell composition and metabolism in ways that may counter age-related immune decline ("inflammaging"). However, a 2025 systematic review of randomized trials concluded there is **insufficient evidence** that UA improves muscle strength, muscle mass, or physical performance — it's well tolerated, but the benefits aren't established yet.

**Taurine** took a hit: a high-profile analysis found circulating taurine does *not* reliably decline with age (it even rose in women), undercutting the idea that taurine is a good aging biomarker or an easy longevity supplement. Human trials remain inconclusive.

- Favor interventions backed by human RCTs over anecdote.
- Treat single-supplement "longevity" claims with healthy skepticism — most are still unproven.

sources.title: Effect of the mitophagy inducer urolithin A on age-related immune decline: a randomized, placebo-controlled trial
Urolithin A supplementation on muscle strength, mass and physical performance: a systematic review

sections.body:
The evidence keeps favoring HIIT as a **time-efficient** way to improve fitness and body composition in women.

- A 2025 systematic review and meta-analysis in recreationally active females found HIIT significantly **improves cardiorespiratory fitness (VO2max)** and **reduces body-fat percentage** across age groups.
- In adolescent women, 6–8 week programs (two sessions/week of ~30-second efforts at 90–95% of max heart rate with active recovery) improved BMI, body fat, lipid profile, blood pressure, and VO2max.
- In postmenopausal women with overweight/obesity, a 12-week HIIT program reduced fat mass and improved insulin sensitivity (HOMA-IR) and lipids.

Practical note: ramp volume gradually and allow recovery — intensity is the active ingredient, but consistency and adequate rest drive results.

sources.title: Effects of HIIT on physical fitness and body composition in recreationally active females: systematic review & meta-analysis
HIIT: impact of duration on body composition, cardiometabolic health and aerobic capacity in adolescent women
HIIT and concurrent training in postmenopausal women with overweight/obesity: a clinical trial

sections.body:
**Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT)** remains the evidence-based first-line, conservative treatment for urinary incontinence.

- A recent meta-analysis in postmenopausal women estimated a **~92% probability of significant improvement** in incontinence symptoms with PFMT versus controls.
- A 2025 systematic review found PFMT effective to prevent and treat incontinence in postpartum women, with **biofeedback** adding benefit for muscle strength.
- Across postmenopausal training studies, effective protocols clustered around **~3 sessions/week for ~10 weeks**, and 14 of 15 studies showed improved pelvic-floor function.

Key point: *technique and consistency matter more than sheer reps* — correct activation (ideally confirmed with a clinician or biofeedback) is what drives results.

sources.title: Effect of pelvic floor muscle training on urinary incontinence in postmenopausal women: systematic review & meta-analysis
PFMT to prevent and treat urinary incontinence in postpartum primiparous women: a systematic review
Training interventions to improve pelvic floor muscle function in postmenopausal women: a systematic review

sections.body:
For **HER2-positive breast cancer**, the big recent news is **DESTINY-Breast09** (phase 3, published in the *New England Journal of Medicine*, Oct 2025): as first-line therapy, the antibody-drug conjugate **trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) plus pertuzumab** nearly doubled median progression-free survival versus the long-standing standard (THP) — about **40.7 vs 26.9 months** — and is expected to change first-line practice.

For **TP53 / p53-mutant** cancers, p53 is still considered "undruggable" and **no p53-targeted therapy is yet approved**. The most advanced candidates are mutation-specific: **rezatapopt (PC14586)**, a reactivator for the TP53 Y220C mutation, is in phase 1/2 (PYNNACLE), and a T-cell engager targeting the R175H mutation entered phase 1 in 2025. These remain early-stage.

Important framing: "alternative" should mean **evidence-based** options discussed with an oncologist — not replacements for standard care.

sources.title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan plus Pertuzumab for HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer (DESTINY-Breast09)
Emerging Therapeutic Strategies for Targeting Tumors With TP53 Mutations
Evaluation of PC14586 (rezatapopt) in advanced solid tumors with a TP53 Y220C mutation (PYNNACLE)

sections.body:
The strongest evidence still points to behavior over supplements for disjointed or restless sleep.

- **CBT-I (cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia)** is the guideline-recommended first-line treatment, improving sleep efficiency ~10–20% with large, durable effects — roughly **5–7x more effective** than supplement approaches.
- **Magnesium** (bisglycinate): a 2025 randomized trial showed only a **modest** improvement in insomnia severity, much of it attributable to placebo (small effect size). Reasonable to try, but not a cure.
- **Melatonin**: the AASM advises **against routine use for chronic insomnia**; it's most useful for circadian issues like jet lag or shift work, and low doses are typically sufficient.

Best-practice habits:

1. Fixed **wake time**, even on weekends.
2. Use the bed **only for sleep**; cool, dark, quiet room.
3. Dim lights and cut screens during a wind-down window.
4. If problems persist, see a provider to rule out conditions like sleep apnea and consider CBT-I.

sources.title: Missing the mark with melatonin: finding the best treatment for insomnia
Management of Chronic Insomnia Disorder in Adults: ACP Clinical Practice Guideline
Magnesium Bisglycinate Supplementation in Adults Reporting Poor Sleep: a randomized, placebo-controlled trial
